summary: In this 1990 interview, Steve Jobs reflects on the evolution of the personal computer, famously describing it as a 'bicycle of the mind.' He discusses the transition from standalone PCs to interpersonal computing networks, the philosophy behind the Macintosh's user-friendly design, and his belief that true innovators must be both thinkers and doers.
themes: The Intersection of Liberal Arts and Technology

principles.statement: The doers are the major thinkers.
principles.rationale: The people who create industry-changing innovations combine art and science; they work through the hard intellectual problems by actually building the product themselves.
principles.source_quote: The people that really create the things that change this industry are both the thinker and doer in one person.

principles.statement: Market research cannot predict non-incremental jumps.
principles.rationale: Customers can only provide feedback on incremental improvements to existing products, not entirely new paradigms they haven't experienced yet.
principles.source_quote: very rarely can your customers predict something that they don’t even quite know they want yet. As an example no market research could have led to the development of the Macintosh

principles.statement: Computers are a means to an end, not an end in themselves.
principles.rationale: Most people don't want to spend months learning how to use a computer; they just want to use it to accomplish their actual goals.
principles.source_quote: the computer was not an end in itself. It was a means to an end. And so most people didn’t want to learn how to use the computer. They just wanted to use it.

principles.statement: Automation in manufacturing is primarily for quality and speed, not just cost reduction.
principles.rationale: In a technology-based marketplace, decreasing time-to-market and increasing product quality are essential for a company to survive and prosper.
principles.source_quote: the automation isn’t there to lower the cost although it does do that, it’s really there to increase the quality and decrease the time it takes us to get our new product, as an example, to market

mental_models.name: Bicycle of the Mind
mental_models.description: A computer is a tool that amplifies inherent human abilities to spectacular magnitudes, much like a tool amplifies physical abilities.
mental_models.example: Comparing the locomotion efficiency of a condor (which is highly efficient) to a human (which is unimpressive), and then to a human riding a bicycle, which completely blows away the condor.

mental_models.name: Passenger Trains to Automobiles
mental_models.description: The shift from centralized mainframe computers to personal computers is analogous to the shift from public transit to personal vehicles, granting individuals freedom and control over their own destiny.
mental_models.example: Using a PC without having to convince other people (like mainframe operators) that you needed to use it, much like driving your own car instead of taking a passenger train.

mental_models.name: The Three Explosions of Computing
mental_models.description: Viewing the history of the computer industry through the lens of major application breakthroughs that drove mass adoption.
mental_models.example: 1. Spreadsheets (financial planning) in 1977/1982. 2. Desktop publishing in 1985. 3. Interpersonal computing (human-to-human communication) in the 1990s.

heuristics: To make a computer easier to use, you need a more powerful computer in the first place.
Once you make a non-incremental jump in product development, go check your instincts with the marketplace.
The older people want to know how a computer does what it does, but the young people just want to know what it can do.

signature_quotes.text: And so for me, a computer has always been a bicycle of the mind. Something that takes us far beyond our inherent abilities.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why the computer is such an influential and interesting machine.

signature_quotes.text: The doers are the major thinkers. The people that really create the things that change this industry are both the thinker and doer in one person.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the balance between thinking and doing in the early days of the semiconductor and PC industry.

signature_quotes.text: The Macintosh was supposed to be the computer for people that just wanted to use a computer without having to learn how to use one.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the goal and success of the Macintosh.

signature_quotes.text: very rarely can your customers predict something that they don’t even quite know they want yet.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the limitations of market research for breakthrough products.

signature_quotes.text: When we started Apple we were out to build computers for our friends. That was all, no idea of a company.
signature_quotes.context: Answering whether he set out to build a company or change the world.

anti_patterns.description: Relying on market research to invent breakthrough products.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Customers don't know they want something that doesn't exist yet; market research is only good for incremental improvements or verifying instincts after a non-incremental jump is made.
It's easy to take credit for thinking, but true innovation requires working through hard intellectual problems while doing the concrete work, much like Leonardo da Vinci mixing his own paints.

anti_patterns.description: Treating multimedia as the end goal of computing.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: People don't buy computers just for multimedia; they buy them for training or interpersonal communication. Multimedia is just a means to help communicate better.
